steps.title: Spot the punctuation pattern
steps.content: The sentence uses an em dash to set off the appositive “now known as the Maunder Minimum.” The next element—”when sunspots were notably scarce”—is a dependent clause that must be properly connected to the noun phrase it modifies.

steps.title: Choose the em dash to maintain parallel structure
steps.content:
An em dash before “when” (choice A) creates a parallel structure with the earlier em-dash, properly introducing the dependent clause.

Options B, C, and D fail:
  • B omits the necessary connector
  • C incorrectly uses a colon before a dependent clause (colons introduce lists or independent clauses)
  • D uses a comma
, which inconsistently mixes punctuation styles.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
